---
title: Modeling Workers' Return-to-Office Decisions in Australia
author: Shaine Rosewel Matala
format:
  revealjs:
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    number-sections: TRUE
    date: today
    chalkboard: TRUE
    output-file: my_revealjs
  beamer: 
    aspectratio: 32
    theme: CambridgeUS
    colortheme: lily
    slide-level: 2
    colorlinks: TRUE
    toc: TRUE
    classoption: [handout]
    date: last-modified
    output-file: my_beamer
  pdf:
    link-citations: TRUE 
    keep-tex: TRUE
    toc: TRUE
    output-file: my_pdf
bibliography: child/references/references.bib
nocite: |
  @*
pdf-engine: latexmk
pdf-engine-opt: -xelatex
csl: apa-6th-edition.csl
---
```{css, echo=FALSE}
/* Force vertical-only scrolling and wrap text within the viewport */
.reveal .slides section.scrollable {
  overflow-x: hidden !important;
  word-wrap: break-word;
}

/* Ensure lists don't expand past the slide width */
.reveal .slides section.scrollable ul, 
.reveal .slides section.scrollable ol {
  max-width: 100%;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}
```

\pagebreak

# Introduction

::: incremental
-   Working from home is a formal arrangement where employees perform duties without daily physical attendance at the office, instead doing so remotely from a place of work such as the home or a café, at conventional hours or flexibly at other times, enabled by information and communication technologies (@vij-2023). 
-   The COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 paved the way for a significant Work from Home (WfH) uptake across Australia.
:::

## Problem statement {.scrollable}

::: incremental
-   In late 2020 and early 2021, few employers believed that the hybrid model would be sustained beyond 2021, and some strongly rejected WfH as a long-term strategy. 
-   Others recognized the need to adapt in terms of flexibility, while some viewed hybrid arrangements as appropriate only for certain roles. 
-   Managerial employees, on the other hand, were generally more supportive of company policies allowing WfH in the long term (DITRDCSA, 2022). 
-   These differing perspectives may translate into varying workplace policies, including mandates to return to the office.
:::

## Objectives

::: incremental
-  To examine the immediate decision of workers when faced with a mandate to return to the worksite 5+ days a week. In particular, it considers whether 
   -   workers prefer to remain in a WfH arrangement (e.g., seeking a job that allows remote work or leaving their current job) or 
   -   to return fully to in-office work (i.e., complying with the mandate). 
-  To understand the factors associated with this decision, including perception of remote work, commute time, gender, age, presence of children, and industry.
:::

## Related Literature

## Logit Results {.scrollable}

```{r, results='asis'}
library(kableExtra)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(knitr)
model <- readRDS("C:/Users/shain/Projects/243/paper/child/model.rds")
round(as.data.frame(summary(model)$coefficients), 3) %>%
           kable(
           booktabs = TRUE,
           escape = TRUE,
           linesep = "",
           col.names = c("Estimate", "SE", "z", "p-value")) %>%
      kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position",
                    position = "center",
                    font_size = 30,
                    full_width = FALSE)
```

## Log Odds Results {.smaller .scrollable}

```{r}
beta1 <- summary(model)$coefficients['commuting_time','Estimate']
beta2 <- summary(model)$coefficients['I(commuting_time^2)','Estimate']
```

```{r}
#| message: FALSE
df <- cbind(as.data.frame(exp(model$coefficients)), as.data.frame(exp(confint(model))))
colnames(df) <- c("Estimate", "2.5%", "97.5%")
round(df,3) %>% kable(
  booktabs = TRUE,
  escape = TRUE,
  linesep = "") %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position",
                position = "center",
                font_size = 30,
                full_width = FALSE)
```

- The log-odds of deciding to resign gets multiplied by 1.510 for a one unit increase in employees' desired number of days to WfH.  
- The log-odds of deciding to resign gets multiplied by 0.769 for a one unit increase in standardized age. (a decrease in log-odds of resigning among older employees.)  
- The log-odds of deciding to resign gets multiplied by 1.46 if employee has a kid.  
- The log-odds of deciding to resign gets multiplied by $\exp(2 \times `r beta2` \times \text{commuting_time}) \times \exp(`r beta2`) \times \exp(`r beta1`) = \exp(`r 2*beta2` \times \text{commuting_time}) \times `r exp(beta2)*exp(beta1)`$ for a unit increase in standardized commute time.

```{r}
commuting_time <- 5
exp(2*beta2*commuting_time)*exp(beta2)*exp(beta1)
```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

b1 <- beta1
b2 <- beta2

df <- data.frame(time = seq(-3, 2, length.out = 100))

df$odds_ratio <- exp(b1 + 2*b2*df$time + b2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = time, y = odds_ratio)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkgreen", size = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Impact of Commute on Resignation (Actual Data Range)",
       subtitle = "Range limited to -2 to +3 Standard Deviations",
       x = "Commute Time (Standardized)",
       y = "Odds Ratio for +1 Unit Increase") + theme_bw()
```



```{r}
library(ggplot2)

predict_df <- data.frame(
  commuting_time = seq(-3.0, 2.0, length.out = 100),
  daysemployee_work_home = 0,
  age = 0.0,
  with_kids = factor("1", levels = c("0", "1")),
  gender = factor("Male", levels = c("Male", "Female"))
)

predict_df$prob <- predict(model, newdata = predict_df, type = "response")

ggplot(predict_df, aes(x = commuting_time, y = prob)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = mean(predict_df$prob), slope = 0.1, 
              linetype = "dashed", color = "gray") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 0.5)) +
  labs(title = "Probability of Resigning by Commute Time",
       subtitle = "Blue line = Your Model | Dashed line = Linear Reference",
       x = "Commute Time (Standardized: -3 to 2)",
       y = "Probability of Resigning (%)") +
  theme_bw()

```



## Conclusion



::: {layout="[[1, 1, 1]]"}
![R Language](images/RLogo.png){#fig-r-logo .fragment width="75%"}

![RStudio IDE](images/RStudioLogo.png){#fig-rstudio .fragment width="70%"}

![TinyTex](images/TinyTextLogo.png){#fig-tinytex .fragment width="50%"}
:::

::: {.callout-note .fragment}
RStudio v2022.07 and later includes support for editing and preview of Quarto documents.
:::

# Integrated workflow {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

R Markdown and Quarto allow for the seamless integration of narrative text, raw data, and executable code in a single document.

## Simple Markdown syntax {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

| Feature | LaTeX | Quarto |
|:-------------------------|:------------------------|:--------------------|
| [**Bold**]{.fragment fragment-index="1"} | [`\textbf{Heavy}`]{.fragment fragment-index="1"} | [`**Heavy**`]{.fragment fragment-index="1"} |
| [*Italic*]{.fragment fragment-index="2"} | [`\textit{Slanted}`]{.fragment fragment-index="2"} | [`*Slanted*`]{.fragment fragment-index="2"} |
| [[Links](https://quarto.org)]{.fragment fragment-index="3"} | [`\href{url}{text}`]{.fragment fragment-index="3"} | [`[text](url)`]{.fragment fragment-index="3"} |
| [`Code`]{.fragment fragment-index="4"} | [`\texttt{fixed}`]{.fragment fragment-index="4"} | [`` `fixed` ``]{.fragment fragment-index="4"} |
| [Images]{.fragment fragment-index="5"} | [`\includegraphics{...}`]{.fragment fragment-index="5"} | [`![](...)`]{.fragment fragment-index="5"} |

: LaTeX vs Markdown {#tbl-comparison}

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
> **Verdict:** Less typing, more presenting for Quarto.
:::

## Inline and Display Math {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

::: incremental
-   In physics, the mass-energy equivalence is stated by the equation $E=mc^2$, discovered in 1905 by Albert Einstein.

-   $$
    \frac{\partial \mathrm C}{ \partial \mathrm t } + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2} \mathrm S^{2}
    \frac{\partial^{2} \mathrm C}{\partial \mathrm S^2}
      + \mathrm r \mathrm S \frac{\partial \mathrm C}{\partial \mathrm S}\ =
      \mathrm r \mathrm C 
    $$ {#eq-black-scholes}

-   $$
    \begin{aligned}
    f(x) &= a(x - h)^2 + k \\
         &= a(x^2 - 2xh + h^2) + k
    \end{aligned}
    $$ {#eq-vertex-expansion}
:::

## [Code execution control]((https://quarto.org/docs/computations/execution-options.html)) {.scrollable background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

::: incremental
-   **`echo`**: **Show the source code.** If `FALSE`, the code stays hidden.

    -   `#| echo: TRUE`
    

    ```{r}
    #| echo: TRUE
    print("I want to show the codes.")
    ```

    -   `#| echo: FALSE`

    ```{r}
    #| echo: FALSE
    print("I want only the result.")
    ```

-   **`eval`**: **Evaluate the code.** If `FALSE`, the code is never executed.

    -   `#| eval: TRUE`

    ```{r}
    #| eval: TRUE
    print("Run the code.")
    ```

    -   `#| eval: FALSE`

    ```{r}
    #| eval: FALSE
    print("Don't run the code.")
    ```
:::

# Dynamic updating {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

Results, tables, and figures update automatically when the underlying data changes, eliminating manual editing errors and ensuring the final paper remains up to date with the analysis.

## Input {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| code-line-numbers: "|1-4|6-8|10-15|1,4"
# load data
library("kableExtra")
data(iris)
iris2 <- iris[4:6, c(1, 3, 2, 4, 5)]

# calculations
mean_sepal_length <- mean(iris2$Sepal.Length)
mean_sepal_width <- mean(iris2$Sepal.Width)

# format into a pretty table
names(iris2) <- gsub('[.].+', '', names(iris2))
rownames(iris2) <- NULL
iris_table <- kable(iris2, booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  add_header_above(c("Length" = 2, "Width" = 2, " " = 1)) %>%
  add_header_above(c("Measurements" = 4, "More attributes" = 1))
```

## Output {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

The means of sepal length and sepal width are `r round(mean_sepal_length,2)` and `r round(mean_sepal_width,2)`, respectively.

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| label: tbl-iris
#| tbl-cap: "Iris Data"
iris_table
```

# Multi-format outputs {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

One source file can be rendered into professional PDFs, Word documents or Tex files for collaborators, interactive HTML reports and presentations, as well as into beamer or PowerPoint.

## [Flexibility](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html) {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

::: incremental
1.  Specify the formats in your YAML.
2.  Use the `Render` button or the terminal (`quarto render your_qmd.qmd --to format`).

-   try for beamer
-   try for html
-   try for pdf
:::

# Automated elements {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

Complex tasks such as cross-referencing (See @sec-cross-referencing, see [Cross-referencing](#sec-cross-referencing)), bibliography management (See @sec-citations), journal formats (See @sec-journal-extensions), plots and table formatting are handled programmatically, which maintains consistency throughout long documents.

## [Cross-referencing](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/cross-references.html) {#sec-cross-referencing background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

| Cross-reference | Text |
|:---------------------------|:-------------------------------------------|
| [**Equations**]{.fragment fragment-index="1"} | [Black-Scholes (@eq-black-scholes) is a mathematical model... @eq-vertex-expansion shows...]{.fragment fragment-index="1"} |
| [**Image**]{.fragment fragment-index="2"} | [See @fig-rstudio]{.fragment fragment-index="2"} |
| [**Tables**]{.fragment fragment-index="3"} | [@tbl-comparison shows how LaTeX differs from Markdown. The data is shown in @tbl-iris]{.fragment fragment-index="3"} |

: {tbl-colwidths="\[20,80\]"}

## [Citations](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html) {#sec-citations background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

::: incremental
-   Bibliography file (`.bib`)
-   Syntax: According to `@klein` -\> According to
-   YAML
    -   `link-citations: TRUE`
    -   `nocite` key in YAML
-   Citation style (`.csl`)
:::

## Extensions for journals {#sec-journal-extensions background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

[JASA](https://github.com/quarto-journals/jasa#readme)

# Challenges {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

::: incremental
-   Debugging
    -   error messages can be vague (especially YAML + LaTeX)
    -   one change may silently override defaults
-   Formatting differences across outputs
    -   looks great in RevealJS but breaks in PDF
    -   fonts, spacing, figure placement behave differently
-   File organization
    -   managing images, references, and extensions can get messy
:::

# References {background-image="images/bg.jpg" background-size="contain" background-opacity="0.5"}

::: {#refs}
:::
